text: The surgeon places incisions in a vertical line along the left rectus muscle, leaving space below the costal margin for potential placement of an additional trocar.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator for gentle and blunt dissection, revealing the sigmoid colon tethered to the right lower quadrant. The dissection progresses, allowing the terminal ileum to be identified as it enters the cecum.

The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix by observing the coalescence of the tinea. A small fissure in the inflamed tissue reveals the body of the appendix. Using the suction irrigator, the surgeon continues dissection, separating the ligament of Treves from the appendix and gradually elevating the appendix from the surrounding inflammatory tissue.

The surgeon uses curved monopolar scissors to dissect the remaining portion of the appendix, creating a space between the appendix and the mesoappendix. The mesoappendix is visible on the left side. The bipolar instrument is used to cauterize the appendiceal artery, which is then transected with the monopolar scissors. Some bleeding occurs but is quickly controlled.
The robotic console's wrists allow the surgeon to operate at an angle within the dissected cavity, as the remainder of the base of the appendix is bluntly dissected from the cecum.

The surgeon ties three interrupted absorbable sutures around the base of the appendix, avoiding imbrication within the inflamed cecum. The inflamed tissue precludes the use of a purse string suture or a stapler for transection.

The surgeon places two simple sutures at the base of the appendix without imbricating the stump. The surgical field is then irrigated to remove clots, followed by irrigation of the dependent portions of the pelvis.

The surgeon deploys an endo-catch bag from the superior port site, which may require dilation. A drain is introduced through the inferior port site, draped into the pelvis and over the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the dilated extraction port at the fascia using a vicryl suture.
